hints.but: 'But' is 'sed' or 'autem' — 'etenim' is explanatory, not adversative

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Lentulum' — 'Lentulus' with -us is nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Lentule' — this is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Lentuli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Lentuli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Lentuli' ends in -i

hints.nominative: 'Nomen' can be nominative, but here it is the object of 'putavit' — accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'suo nomine' — 'suum nomen' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'sui nominis' — 'suum nomen' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'nominis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'fatalis' — 'fatalem' with -em is accusative

steps.prompt: In 'nomen fatalem putavit,' what construction is at work?

hints.accusative + infinitive: There is no infinitive here — 'putavit' takes a double accusative (object + complement)
hints.accusative + ablative: No ablative is present — both 'nomen' and 'fatalem' are accusative
hints.accusative + dative: No dative is present — 'putare' takes a double accusative construction

hints.to prune: 'Putare' literally means 'to prune' but idiomatically means 'to think/consider'
hints.to compute: 'To compute' is 'computare' — 'putare' means to think or consider

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'putat' — 'putavit' with -av- perfect stem is perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'putabat' — 'putavit' is perfect indicative
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'putaverat' — 'putavit' is perfect indicative

hints.punio, punire, punivi, punitum: These are the parts of 'punire' (to punish)
hints.pono, ponere, posui, positum: These are the parts of 'ponere' (to place)
hints.peto, petere, petivi, petitum: These are the parts of 'petere' (to seek)

steps.prompt: What is the historical allusion in Lentulus considering his 'nomen fatalem'?
steps.target: Lentulus believed a Sibylline prophecy that a third Cornelius would rule Rome
choices: Lentulus believed a Sibylline prophecy that a third Cornelius would rule Rome
he wanted to change his name
his name appeared in a will

hints.his family name was cursed: The name was not cursed — Lentulus believed prophecy destined a 'Cornelius' for power
hints.he wanted to change his name: He did not want to change it — he took pride in it as prophetically significant
hints.his name appeared in a will: No will is involved — the Sibylline books allegedly predicted a third 'Cornelius' (after Cinna and Sulla)

translation: For indeed if P. Lentulus thought his name fated
